svg.title: Domain Reputation vs IP Reputation
svg.desc: Deep comparison of domain reputation and IP reputation in email deliverability. IP reputation is a legacy, secondary signal shared across all senders on the same IP address, influenced by complaint rates, spam trap hits, volume patterns, and blocklist status. It resets when switching ESPs and is diluted by other senders on shared infrastructure. Domain reputation is the primary modern signal, unique to the sender's domain and portable across ESPs. It is influenced by engagement rate, authentication alignment, content quality, complaint ratio, and domain age. Domain reputation carries increasing weight in modern filtering while IP reputation carries declining weight. Both signals combine into a reputation score that determines whether messages reach the inbox, promotions tab, or spam folder.

cc:Work.dc:title: Domain Reputation vs IP Reputation Comparison
cc:Work.dc:creator: InboxAlly
cc:Work.dc:subject: domain reputation, IP reputation, email deliverability, shared IP, dedicated IP, ESP migration, SPF, DKIM, DMARC, engagement rate, complaint ratio, spam traps, blocklists, inbox placement, spam filtering, sender reputation
cc:Work.dc:description: Side-by-side comparison showing how domain reputation (primary, portable, sender-controlled) and IP reputation (secondary, shared, ESP-dependent) differ across five dimensions and combine into filtering decisions.

g.aria-label: IP Reputation: secondary signal, shared across senders, declining weight in modern filtering
g.data-description: Legacy reputation signal based on sending IP address, shared across all senders on the same infrastructure
g.text: Shared across all senders on IP
Multiple senders share the same IP reputation
Aggregate behavior across all senders on IP
New IP = new reputation to build from scratch
Shared IPs mean others can damage your reputation
Switching ESPs can reset IP reputation
but does not fix underlying sending problems

g.aria-label: Domain Reputation: primary signal, unique to sender, increasing weight in modern filtering
g.data-description: Primary modern reputation signal based on sending domain, unique to the sender and portable across infrastructure
g.text: Your domain, your reputation — nobody else's
Signals you directly control through sending practices
Switching providers does not reset domain reputation
No other sender can affect your domain reputation
Google, Microsoft, Yahoo increasingly prioritize domain signals
A damaged domain follows you everywhere
Fixing domain reputation requires fixing sending behavior

g.aria-label: Reputation score: combined from domain reputation (primary weight) and IP reputation (secondary weight)
g.data-description: Combined reputation score weighted toward domain reputation
Strong reputation results in inbox placement

g.aria-label: Outcome: Message delivered to promotions tab
g.data-description: Marketing content categorized into promotions tab

g.aria-label: Outcome: Message delivered to spam folder
g.data-description: Poor reputation results in spam folder placement
